topic: Why Mercury Gets So Hot During The Day
title: Why the Closest Planet to the Sun Still Cooks and Cools Fast

imageCredit.creator: UCL Mathematical & Physical Sciences from London, UK

lesson.learn: Mercury is scorched by direct sunlight because it orbits closer to the Sun than any other planet, but distance alone does not explain everything. Mercury has almost no atmosphere, so there is very little to spread heat around or hold it in at night. That means the dayside can become extremely hot while the nightside drops to frigid temperatures. The result is one of the most dramatic temperature swings in the Solar System.
lesson.simpler: Mercury gets so hot because it is close to the Sun and does not have an atmosphere to balance the heat.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Mercury's temperature swings are extreme because it is sunlit directly, but it lacks the atmosphere needed to spread or retain heat.

learnImageCredit.creator: NASA Goddard Space Flight Center from Greenbelt, MD, USA

lesson.deeperDive: Mercury's day side becomes hot mainly because sunlight lands on an airless surface and gets absorbed by rock and regolith. Without a substantial atmosphere, there is no weather system to move energy from place to place, and almost no greenhouse effect to trap or redistribute heat. Mercury also rotates slowly enough that a single day lasts far longer than its year-long orbit would suggest, which gives one side a long time to bake and then a long time to cool. That is why Mercury is a planet of extremes rather than constant heat. It is not the hottest planet overall, though: Venus wins that title because its atmosphere traps heat far more efficiently.
lesson.funFact: A single sunrise-to-sunrise day on Mercury lasts about 176 Earth days.
